headline: pki.csr.sign issues PKCS#10 certification requests -- self-signed by the subject key for proof of possession, over every signature algorithm the toolkit supports.
summary: The enrollment producing side. pki.csr.sign builds a CertificationRequestInfo, signs it with the subject's own private key (a certification request has no issuer -- it proves possession of the private half of the certified key), and emits a CertificationRequest (RFC 2986) that pki.schema.csr.parse, OpenSSL, and a CA enrollment pipeline all accept. A spec describes the request -- the subject (a common-name string, an array of relative distinguished names, or raw Name DER, and it may be empty), the public key being certified, an optional extensionRequest carrying the requested v3 extensions, and an optional challengePassword. The requested extensions -- subject alternative names, key usage, extended key usage, basic constraints, certificate policies, and subject key identifier, or an array of pre-encoded Extension DER -- are the v3 extensions a certificate authority copies into the issued certificate, carried in the PKCS#9 extensionRequest attribute (RFC 2985). The signature algorithm is resolved from the subject key through the same registry the certificate and CMS signers use, so RSA (PKCS#1 v1.5 or PSS), ECDSA, EdDSA, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, and the composite arms all sign without a per-algorithm branch. The version is the mandatory bare INTEGER 0, the attributes are the required implicit SET-OF (empty when none are requested, its members DER-sorted), and the proof-of-possession signature is verified against the subject public key before the request is returned -- exactly what openssl req -verify checks. Returns DER, or a PEM CERTIFICATE REQUEST with opts.pem; malformed input throws a typed CsrError.

Added: pki.csr.sign(spec, key, opts) builds and signs a PKCS#10 certification request -- self-signed by the subject's own key for proof of possession -- and returns DER, or a PEM CERTIFICATE REQUEST with opts.pem. The subject may be empty; requested v3 extensions (subject alternative names, key usage, extended key usage, basic constraints, certificate policies, subject key identifier, or pre-encoded Extension DER) ride in a PKCS#9 extensionRequest attribute a CA copies into the issued certificate, and an optional challengePassword is carried too. The signing algorithm is resolved from the subject key: RSA PKCS#1 v1.5 / PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, or a composite arm. Request parsing remains pki.schema.csr.parse.
Fixed: The certificate and certification-request distinguished-name and extension encoders now reject an unrecognized attribute, extended-key-usage purpose, or certificate-policy name at build time with a typed error, rather than emitting a malformed object identifier -- an unknown name in a pki.x509.sign or pki.csr.sign spec fails closed instead of producing an unparseable structure.
